posts.caption:
In the early 2010s, #Topshop was one of the hottest brands to wear to the #MetGala, dressing all the rising “It girls.” Over a decade later, fast fashion is borrowing more than ever from the credibility and visibility of luxury spaces like the Met, as a powerful marketing platform.
When a celebrity wears something like Zara by #JohnGalliano or Gap by #ZacPosen, it blurs the line between mass and luxury. Obviously, these dresses aren’t coming from the same places as the jeans and T-shirts we buy in stores, but they still function as cultural symbols and marketing tools.
Whether celebrities should wear these brands depends on what you think the Met Gala is meant to represent. On one hand, it reflects the current reality of fashion. On the other, it risks turning the event into a marketing vehicle for mass retailers and diluting the focus on craftsmanship and artistry that traditionally defined the gala.
What do you think?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 07, 2026. May be an image of dress, magazine and text that says 'Should Celebs Wear "Fast Fashion" to the Met Gala?'.

posts.caption:
#PhilipTreacy’s ship hat is probably one of the most beautiful pieces of millinery I’ve ever seen, and #Madonna wearing it to the 2026 #MetGala was the perfect excuse to talk about it again.
Philip made the hat for Isabella Blow in 1994. He was inspired by the 18th-century Coiffure à la Belle-Poule, a nautical hairstyle that featured a model ship sailing atop a sea of thick, sculpted hair.
In 1995, #MichaelJackson reportedly offered $25,000 for the hat; however, Philip refused as he only had one. He said “even though I love Michael Jackson, I just didn’t want it going off to Neverland.” The piece went on to become an iconic moment in fashion history. It inspired designers like #JeanPaulGaultier, who revived the headpiece again for his 2025 couture collection in collaboration with Ludovic de Saint Sernin
Image courtesy of Gallica Bibliothèque Nationale de France / Kieran Doherty / Ram Shergill / Ricardo Gomes

posts.caption:
Not every bridal detail is chosen to stand out loudly.
Some are noticed in quieter moments… while holding a bouquet, adjusting a veil, exchanging rings, or capturing memories that stay forever. ✨
Bridal nails are one of those details. Beyond the polish, the embellishments, and carefully chosen shades… they become part of every gesture throughout the celebration.
From soft close-up shots during the mehendi to the delicate moments of slipping on rings, bridal nails quietly complete the entire look without ever asking for attention.
And as bridal fashion continues to evolve, nail trends are becoming far more intentional… moving beyond classic reds and neutrals into styles that feel personal, modern, and moodboard-worthy.
Some brides are leaning towards glazed finishes and milky tones that feel soft, minimal, and timeless. Others are embracing pearl accents, chrome details, 3D textures, delicate florals, or subtle shimmer that catches beautifully under every light.
There are nail designs created to make a statement in every photograph… and there are understated styles that feel effortlessly elegant, blending seamlessly into the bridal aesthetic.
Think of bridal nails as more than just a finishing touch… they’re tiny details that become part of some of the most photographed moments of your wedding story.
Because while the outfit may draw the first glance… it’s often these little details that make the entire look feel unforgettable. 💫
Which bridal nail trend would you save for your big day? ✨

posts.caption:
Some wedding moments feel less like photographs… and more like memories unfolding in real time. ✨
Under open skies, surrounded by petals that seemed to dance through the air, this felt like one of those rare moments where joy became almost tangible. Nothing about it was overly posed or carefully contained… it was loud with laughter, soft with emotion, and beautiful in the most effortless way.
There was something incredibly comforting in the way they looked at each other amidst all the celebration around them… as if even in the middle of chaos, they had quietly found their calm in one another. In his smile, in the way she couldn’t stop laughing, in the warmth that surrounded them from every side… you could feel a love that wasn’t trying to impress anyone, only fully living the moment exactly as it came.
And then came the details that made everything feel almost surreal… petals suspended mid-air, pastel drapes moving gently with the breeze, loved ones gathered around with the kind of happiness that can’t be rehearsed. Every frame carried movement, emotion, and the kind of energy that makes a memory stay alive long after the day is over. 💫
Because sometimes, the most unforgettable wedding moments aren’t the perfectly composed ones.
They’re the ones overflowing with feeling… the kind you can almost hear, even through a photograph.

posts.caption:
Some weddings don’t rely on grandeur to leave an impression… they do it through softness, intention, and the way every little detail feels deeply personal. ✨
From the first look of wonder on her bridesmaids’ faces to the quiet smiles exchanged beneath a sky filled with petals, every moment here carried a kind of warmth that felt beautifully unfiltered. Nothing seemed rushed, nothing overly staged… just emotions unfolding naturally, exactly the way the best celebrations do.
There was an elegance in how everything came together so effortlessly. The ivory architecture, delicate pastel florals, subtle hints of peach and blush woven through the décor… every corner felt calm, romantic, and timeless all at once. Even the smallest details — the floral arrangements, the intimate seating, the soft textures blended into the celebration without ever asking for attention too loudly.
And then there were the people. 🤍
The bridesmaids reacting with pure excitement, friends gathering around with laughter that instantly filled the space, and a couple so lost in the joy of the moment that the rest of the world almost seemed to disappear around them. You could feel how loved they were in every frame.
Some weddings are remembered for extravagance.
And some are remembered because they felt like home… wrapped in pastel hues, happy chaos, and moments so genuine they almost move even in still photographs. 💫

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on March 14, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and text that says 'Dear World, Fashion Styling Is a Job. IsaJob.NotaHobby. Not a Hobby. StyistElkir Stylist StylistElixir Elixir'.

posts.caption: If something needs such in-depth analysis to be understood than the assignment was a failure! It’s not a dissertation on the politics of 17th century France. It’s CLOTHING! It’s a fashion show and since fashion shows have been able to be seen by millions and consumed in photos and video for decades, those who are not in the loop to see the clothes for real are only left with what we see and to dare to say that just because a design has “References” or inner history negates it from being judged as what it simply LOOKS LIKE, is insultingly arrogant, something that takes 4,000 hours of the most intricate and elaborate technique… CAN STILL LOOK HORRID! All that time does not acquit it of its ugliness or stupidity of design! When all that history and back story sgd references WORK IN CONJUNCTION with the clothes to crest something magical (Blazy/Chanel) then the framework of all that in depth research and application of history is breathtaking, when it’s like JWA/Dior, it just comes across as over focus on ideas that then translate into nothing more than dissertations on a thought than the true purpose of what is happening… MAKING BEAUTIFUL CLOTHES! And for you to suggest that unless you’re erudite and informed of history that you shouldn’t comment is the height of condescension! Anyone can see! And no matter how layered the meaning, if it looks like all that time taken to produce garbage still looks like GARBAGE, it changes not its beauty because you have the knowing of the thought processes and HISTOIRE D’MAISON behind you! It’s pretentious and asinine
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on January 27, 2026.

posts.caption:
A fashion stylist’s day is mostly not styling.
It is answering the same PR email for the fifth time. Packing, unpacking, repacking.
Explaining to a client why their piece is stuck in customs. Waiting for a sample that is still in Paris.
Worrying about a loan that has not come through. Reworking the look because the talent decided they hate blue. Chasing a courier down the street for your package. Tracking down one pair of shoes in a specific size.
The styling is the part that gets seen. The styling is maybe ten percent of the day.
Anyone who has done this job knows.
Comment STYLIST to find out how we can help.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on May 10, 2026. May be a graphic of text that says 'What a Fashion Stylist Actually Does All Day. STYLISTELIXIR LIXIR STYLIST'.

posts.caption:
Here is what a professional fashion styling job should include from the client side.
Payment for the full job, not just the shoot day. A contract before the work starts. A name on the credits. Respect for the no when a stylist says it. Kit fees that actually cover the kit. A career that compounds instead of resetting every January.
Most of this list is not radical. It is the standard in every other professional industry. The fact that it reads as aspirational in fashion says more about fashion than it does about the list.
These are the basics that every other industry takes for granted. The contract. The credit. The payment. The respect for the boundary.
The industry will not change on its own. The standard rises when these become the floor, not the ceiling.One contract, one credit, one paid invoice at a time.
Comment STYLIST to find out how Stylist Elixir helps fashion stylists build careers on these standards.
With love, Em x

posts.caption:
Every stylist has been asked when they are going to get a real job.
The asking has not stopped because the answer has not landed yet. It will.
The invoices are not a side hustle. The portfolio is not a hobby. The training did not happen in a classroom but it happened. Years of it. On set. In showrooms. In hotel hallways. In customs offices. In the quiet hours after the shoot when nobody else is paying attention.
The industry knows it is a career. The contracts know. The agents know. The fact that the
people outside the industry are still catching up does not change what the work has always
A career is what you build when nobody hands you one. There is no clear path into styling. No certification. No straight line. That does not make it less of a career. It makes it more.
To every stylist who has had to defend the job at a family dinner, in a friend’s group chat, in a comment from someone who has never done it. The work speaks for itself eventually.
Comment STYLIST to find out how Stylist Elixir helps fashion stylists build careers that stop needing to be explained.
With love, Em x
Can you related? Do you get asked?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on May 08, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and text that says 'Fashion Styling Is a Real Career. STYLIST STYLISTELIXIR LIXIR'.

posts.caption:
There are things about being a fashion stylist that nobody outside the job will fully understand.
The hours spent looking for one specific thing. The cab ride home with eight bags. The returns that take longer than the shoot. The cry in the back of the taxi after a job that nobody knew was almost a disaster.
The pride of a pinned hem nobody will ever see. The PR email that reads friendly but is not. The way a stylist’s brain does not switch off, even at a dinner party.
This is the post for the stylists who recognise themselves in this. Save it. Send it to the friend
who will laugh. Send it to the assistant who is just starting and is wondering if it is supposed to
feel like this. It is.
The career looks glamorous and feels like work. Both are true. Anyone who says it is only one is wrong.
Comment STYLIST to be in a room with stylists who get it.
With love, Em x

posts.caption:
People gossip about who “looks gay” who looks butch who looks like a dyke who looks “slutty” who is trying too hard who is visibly trans. The judgement happens near-constantly. And not everyone gets read the same way or survives it equally.
For queer folx makeup and gender expression are not fixed identity markers. They are decisions made in motion: what to wear, where to wear it, when to soften it and when to let it take up space.
Even within queer spaces some people have more room to experiment than others. For queer and trans people who grew up before fluidity became marketable, visibility has rarely been just trend or self-expression.
It has been a balancing act with safety expectations and the cost of being seen.
Read more on the link in bio
By Tejaswi Subramanian

posts.caption:
Every bar calendar now seems to have a guest shift on it.
What began as bartender swaps has become India’s new cocktail flex: visiting mixologists, limited-edition menus, global bar names and alcohol brands finding a smoother way into the craft conversation.
Awards gave bartenders star power. Brands found a marketing format that feels less like a hard sell. Drinkers got novelty without abandoning the comfort of a familiar bar.
The catch is obvious. When every night is programmed to feel special, special starts to feel scheduled. A bar can become known for hosting everyone else before it becomes known for itself.
Novelty brings people in. Comfort brings them back.
Read the full story at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#BarTakeover #CocktailCulture #IndiaBars #GuestShift #PremiumAlcohol

posts.caption:
At the Met Gala 2026, fashion has been framed as art, and celebrities have showcased clothing as material, labour, and object.
Karan Johar (@karanjohar) in Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05) treats the body as canvas, with painted and embroidered surfaces drawing from Raja Ravi Varma, while Malhotra’s own bandhgala foregrounds labour, displaying the process as a part of the final piece.
Siblings Gauravi Kumari (@gauravikumari) and Padmanabh Singh in Prabal Gurung (@pachojaipur) have built directly from archive and atelier. Gauravi’s look incorporates her grandmother, Maharani Gayatri Devi’s chiffon sari into a couture structure that preserves its drape, while Padmanabh’s Phulghar coat, developed over 600+ hours in velvet with aari, zardozi, dabka and resham work, turns Rajasthani craft into surface and object.
Natasha Poonawalla (@natasha.poonawalla) in Dolce & Gabbana × Marc Quinn shifts fashion into sculpture, centring an orchid form as the primary object, with the body acting as its base.
By drawing on craft traditions, art-historical references, and material-led construction, these celebrities have positioned their presence as part of a larger exchange where fashion operates as a legitimate artistic language on an international stage. They’re not merely wearers of couture.
#met2026

Instead of chasing viral moments, fashion houses are doubling down on heritage, technical mastery, and long-term cultural relevance. From archive-driven collaborations to legacy houses securing elite creative talent, the message is clear: stability is the new aspiration.
Brands are building worlds now, not just products. In a market flooded with trend cycles and AI imitation, provenance has become the real luxury.

posts.caption:
In case your weekend plans are loose and you need something to be insufferable about: Vintage fashion refers to clothing that is at least 20 years old, defined solely by its age. Archival fashion refers to specific, rare, and culturally significant items from a designer’s past collection—the grails, the runway moments, the pieces people spend years hunting down.
While all archival pieces may eventually become vintage, not all vintage items are considered archival.
The more you know. Happy weekending.
#VintageFashion #ArchivalFashion #FashionHistory #TheCitizensPoste #Moodboard

@dairyboy by @paigelorenze has seriously mastered the art of a creative campaign. Sun-faded textures, animal motifs, worn-in textiles, Scottish plaids… it all feels intentionally nostalgic without slipping fully into costume.
The Farm Friends campaign leans into the imperfections of country life—livestock, greenery, natural lighting, slightly undone styling—and ends up feeling far more lived-in than the overly polished cottagecore aesthetic that’s flooded the internet for the past few years. Makes me reconsider city life.

posts.caption:
I know everyone is still locked into Met Gala discourse, but we can’t forget to wish a happy belated birthday to the late Audrey Hepburn.
Hepburn completely shifted the fashion landscape of the 1950s and 60s away from excess and toward something cleaner, sharper, and infinitely more timeless. Through her partnership with Hubert de Givenchy, she helped define a new kind of elegance built on simplicity, tailoring, and restraint—most famously in Breakfast at Tiffany’s and that iconic black dress.
What made her style endure wasn’t trend-chasing. It was consistency. She understood personal style before the internet turned it into content.
She’ll always be our girl.
#AudreyHepburn #Givenchy #FashionHistory #BreakfastAtTiffanys #FashionFilm

posts.caption:
Not every look killed the 2026 Met Gala, but there were definitely a few that understood the assignment.
Rihanna, SZA, Beyoncé, Madonna, Anok Yai, Hunter Schafer, and Miles Chamley-Watson all delivered—and I was especially relieved to see Maison Margiela back on the carpet, plus a few looks that actually leaned into costume and performance instead of playing it safe.
Not perfect, but at least some people got the memo.
Who did I miss?
#MetGala #CostumeArt #FashionIsArt #RedCarpet #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
Initial read on the 2026 Met Gala is giving a disconnect between concept and execution. A theme as expansive as “Costume Art” requires clarity of interpretation—and so far, that clarity feels uneven.
We got off to a bit of a rough start. Let’s be real: attendees aren’t really dressing on theme. Yes, there are referential looks (and those matter), but referencing something isn’t the same as executing it well. Right now, it’s reading more underwhelming than intentional.
That said, there are highlights. Robert Wun is consistently delivering, and Beyoncé is officially back after a decade, attending with Blue Ivy Carter, both in custom Olivier Rousteing. The moment we’ve all been waiting for.
Some strong moments—just not enough of them yet. More coverage soon.
#MetGala #CostumeArt #FashionIsArt #TheCitizensPoste #Beyoncé

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Growing up in Chennai as the youngest of three siblings, acting wasn’t part of the plan for model student Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). During her gap year after college, the then 21-year-old dabbled in modelling and ended up at a film audition for kicks. “I thought if I do get it, I’ll do it for a year and then go back to studying,” she says. “The idea was to do it temporarily, never make it a career. I’m sure my teachers were disappointed when I became an actor. I think some of them still are,” she laughs.
We’re not the first to ask her what her alternative career would be, but this time she has an answer: “I’m doing it now,” she says with excitement. “I think this entrepreneurial journey is what I’ve always wanted to do,” says the serial entrepreneur, whose work straddles films, beauty, wellness, fashion, food, and sport, all at once.
For our second anniversary issue, @chloechou13 flew to Hyderabad to chat with our cover star. Full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Samantha Ruth Prabhu’s (@samantharuthprabhuoffl) face card is immaculate. We notice this on a Friday morning, when we are on the set of our May cover shoot in Mumbai, and the star is getting ready for her first shot. At some point, the topic moves on to her upcoming film, and someone in the room asks, “Who’s the hero?” Samantha simply says, “I don’t do those kinds of films anymore.” We can’t help but grin.
It’s been a busy seven days for the star, between promotions and shooting patchwork for her upcoming film ‘Maa Inti Bangaaram’. “The story will be the hero in the movies that I do. The story is bigger than the producer, bigger than the actors in it.”
It isn’t about occupying the lead role on the top of the call sheet, she explains. In her career, Samantha has, in several of her biggest films, played characters orbiting male superstars, and knows all too well that female characters are often relegated to the role of a catalyst in the male protagonist’s journey, to propel his emotional arc as a narrative device. She, however, counterbalanced them with more textured roles, such as the journalist and storyteller Madhuravani in ‘Mahanati’ (2018), the emotionally complex Vaembu in the cult neo-noir ‘Super Deluxe’ (2019), and the 70-year-old trapped in a younger body as Swathi in ‘Oh! Baby’ (2019).
At the link in bio, @chloechou13 in conversation with the star.

posts.caption:
For our second anniversary issue, there was no looking beyond Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). Two years ago, she almost quit acting when she was diagnosed with myositis, an autoimmune condition that causes muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain. It forced her to slow down and taught her a lesson at peak fame that she will never forget. “I was just so focused on running this race for which I don’t even know what the prize at the finish line was,” recalls the actor of the time when everything came to a grinding halt.
Since then, she’s renegotiated everything: her work ethic, priorities, even brand deals. “I realised all the checklists that I was so eager to tick off weren’t my own. They were checklists made for me by other people,” she says. Now at 39, she’s not only a top-billed actor but also an audacious producer, a serial entrepreneur, a fierce investor, and a chatty podcast host. At the link in bio, the multi-hyphenate talks to @chloechou13 about success, silent retreats, swimming, and what is likely to be her joyful next chapter.

posts.caption:
Some pieces in a wardrobe are bought. Some are borrowed. And then there are the ones that become part of family legacy.
The Kanjivaram sari your mother wore at an anniversary dinner. The silk piece your nani folded away for a special occasion. The one that somehow appears in every family wedding album across decades.
And every time the heirloom sari is draped today, every fold, pleat, and pin somehow brings back all the little reminders and life lessons she unknowingly passed down with it.
Through its ‘Threads Of Us’ campaign, VRK Heritage beautifully celebrates this very emotion—how a sari isn’t just worn across generations, but carries the story of the women who loved it before too.

posts.caption:
MET Gala: Beauty’s Starring Role
This year the MET Gala explored fashion as art and beauty took up a starring role. The most experimental and our favourite from the night? Anok Yai’s face, painted in gold with 3D tear drops glistening down referencing the iconic Black Madonna sculpture. Another favourite included Bad Bunny’s take on the “ageing body”. In the age of baby botox, collagen shots and facelifts, he walked up the MET stairs with a cane in tow and an aged-prosthetic face, “53 years” in the making.
Reliance Retail Acquires Priyanka Chopra’s Anomaly
Lifestyle conglomerate Reliance Retail has acquired actor Priyanka Chopra Jonas’s global haircare brand, Anomaly. First launched in 2021, Anomaly has established itself in the global market for its clean and vegan formulations.
Footnote: Birkenstock’s Nail Paint Venture
The 250-year old German footwear company, Birkenstock, which is popular for its orthopedic-focused sandals with a specialised footbed design has launched a glossy range of five vegan, plant-based nail paints crafted to match the colour palette of its Arizona slippers. These polishes, priced at $12 each, are sold alongside a clear base and a top coat.
Maison Margiela’s Couture Fragrances
Drawing inspiration from the world of haute couture, the L’Oréal Group-owned Maison Margiela has recently unveiled a new lineup of artisanal haute fragrances. Titled Scentsorium, the six scents were three years in the making by the luxury maison’s former creative head John Galliano and have been released as an aromatic equivalent of its couture clothing.
SK-II Debuts in India
The luxe legacy beauty label SK-II has finally debuted in the Indian market courtesy LUXASIA, the omnichannel beauty distributor.
Attended by Hollywood actor and global brand ambassador Anna Sawai, the immersive launch event last month further noted a Magic Scan—the AI driven analysis for attendees, exploring skin-age and other concerns.

Using the language of craft, “The Right to Be Seen: Reframing the Passport Photo in Craft” – idea, concept, and direction by The Voice of Fashion; curated by Juhi Pandey for Journeying Across The Himalayas 2025 –is a series built around the concept of the unfiltered identity photograph.
Working with artisan communities across the Indian Himalayan region, the exhibition shifts focus from craft to maker. Each portrait emerges as an interpretation of the self, shaped through an intimate engagement with material and practice.
As The Himalayan Knot travels across continents, presented by the Friday Sari Project Curated Store and Showcase at London Craft Week, a collection of portraits of Raziya Bano – an artisan from Kargil, India – will be on display. Catch it from 12–17 May 2026.

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
To all the mums reaching for their dreams with their little ones in tow…this is for you.
Your kids are watching you, learning from you, seeing your passion and your commitment and your drive and that is EVERYTHING.
As women, we have been doing what we do with our little ones by our side for millennia. Whether that’s running the home, running the office or running the world.
Don’t underestimate how powerful and strong you are.
To all the women waiting for their little ones, I see you and I feel you. Your little ones will come, in time and sometimes in an unexpected way 🫶🏼
I know this can be a hard day depending on your situation, so no matter what, be the kindest mother to yourself 🫶🏼
And take the time to remember to look back to your matrilineal ancestors, what those women did and went through in their lives because that’s where our power comes from.
Happy Mothers Day from here in Australia 🫶🏼

posts.caption:
The most coveted colour in the ancient world came from a sea snail that smelled like garlic and cost more than gold.
250,000 snails to make one ounce of dye and workshops had to be built outside city limits because the smell was unbearable.
A woman spotted wearing the wrong shade at a recital was dragged from the room by Nero’s guards and stripped of her property.
What made it worth all of that wasn’t just the difficulty. Every other ancient dye degraded in sunlight but Tyrian purple deepened. Its permanence  was the actual luxury.
When Constantinople fell in 1453 the knowledge disappeared with the empire. The snails were saved from extinction almost by accident.
Part of The Goddess Project. I’ll also be doing a deep dive on this for my podcast The Culture of Cloth 🔗 link in bio.
Cover image: Rex Harrison as Julius Caesar in Cleopatra (1963).
Quotes from Kassia St Clair, The Secret Lives of Colour, used with her kind permission.

posts.caption: To give more context for those who may not know, the MET gala is a fundraiser for the costume department of the Metropolitan Museum of art in New York, "The MET." The costume department of the MET does not receive any taxpayer dollars, it is completely funded by private donation. As this is a charity event, they need to have attendees who can donate or raise big funds. By getting celebrities to come, they get a lot of media attention. It is an excellent formula for funding this branch of the museum. The Museums in the USA are funded by American citizens, financiers, captains of industry, and artists coming together as is the case with the MET. Everyone who lives in New York City can visit the Metropolitan museum of art for free. It is a fantastic museum, the building is is 2.2 million square feet and yet can only show a portion of it's inventory at one time. The museum has way more than paintings and dresses. It has sculptures, Persian rugs, various pieces of historical and religious art, and a glorious collection of furniture. Although America has many museums, most of them pale in comparison to what you see in Europe. But the MET is one of our crown jewels. It is very expensive to operate and preserve. This type of fundraiser helps to prevent tax burden on the citizens of New York. And yes, I love the MET.

posts.caption:
A few days ago I posted about Mariano Fortuny and promised a full post on Henriette, because she deserved the spotlight rather than the margin.
This is that post.
Everything here traces to a single document…a patent filed in Paris on 10 June 1909, held at the Marciana Library in Venice. Mariano filed it under his name because he was present and the deadline required it. Then he wrote a note on the same document, in his own handwriting, naming Henriette Negrin as the inventor.
That note sat in the archive for 112 years but the V&A only updated their catalogue labels in 2021.
Mariano’s acknowledgement was there from the beginning…it just took the institution a century to follow it.

posts.caption:
Yesterday I posted about the Indus Valley, where cotton was first threaded through copper beads in 6000 BCE, where the draped rectangle became the primary garment of one of the largest civilisations in the ancient world.
That logic travelled west through Mesopotamia, Egypt and Greece. In 1909 it arrived in a palazzo on the Grand Canal in the hands of a man who rowed himself across the Venetian lagoon every morning to make gowns that could not be dated.
Mariano Fortuny called himself a painter but else called him the Magician of Venice. He was severely allergic to horses, obsessed with electricity and spent his life reaching for a father he lost at three years old.
Peggy Guggenheim wore the same Delphos for decades. Isadora Duncan wore it to revive Classical Greek dance. Marcel Proust used it as a symbol of desire, memory, and lost time throughout one of the greatest novels ever written.
The Met @metmuseum chose it to announce this year’s theme. Fashion is Art and Fortuny already knew.
He was reaching for ancient Greece but the tradition he was reviving is older than Greece. It goes back to the Indus Valley and to the origin of cloth.
Part of an ongoing series on fashion, history, and whose stories get told.
Collage work by @juliewolfeartist
@metgalaofficial_ @metcostumeinstitute
#MetGala #CostumeArt #MetCostumeArt #metgala2026 #arthistory

posts.caption:
“I don’t want to understand everyone; I want a world where I live and let others live. In New York City, I was staying in the middle of a huge building, surrounded by people of different races and religions, and there was no tension whatsoever. Sloterdijk even talks about how we need to learn a certain kind of discretion. That’s what bothers me — the idea that I have to understand your world. How am I supposed to understand your world when neither you understand it nor I understand my own? I’m very superficial in that regard. You have a neighbor — establish some kind of order, with basic respect, and don’t push yourself onto them, nor let them push themselves onto you. I take great pleasure in discretion. Maybe that comes with age.”
For the May Issue of Vogue Adria, we spoke with @SlavojZizeks, one of today’s most renowned philosophers, about the future of the left, the dangers facing modern society, one night stands, and revolution.

We surveyed hundreds of Vogue and GQ readers about their wants from #AI in fashion. The key takeaway? Consumers are cautious, and the execution of early applications of AI in fashion is what’s holding them back right now. Those who have adopted its use for shopping are driven primarily by convenience and efficiency, but many are constrained by concerns around trust and authenticity. posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour—the two Mirandas, as it were—in a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to “The Devil Wears Prada.”
“In terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,” Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue’s May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that “The Devil Wears Prada 2” was happening? She called Streep, of course. “I knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,” Wintour says. “I trusted her implicitly.”
Read the full story at the link in bio.
Photographed by @annieleibovitz, Styled by @therealgracecoddington, Hair by @thebobbymichael and @dmchairmakeup, Makeup by @melissasilvermakeup and @dmchairmakeup, Tailoring by @billbulldresses, Produced by AL Studio, Production Design by @not.thestate. Vogue, May 2026.

posts.caption: Hand-me-downs carry more than just style; they hold stories and lessons that shape us. Reflecting on what we inherit from those before us can reveal deeper connections to our own journeys as creators. It’s worth considering how these influences can either empower or weigh us down in the quest for our unique voices.

posts.caption: The city of Huron, California, has named May 3rd Willy Chavarria (@newyorkwilly) Day to honor his contributions to fashion, art, and culture more broadly. The timing is serendipitous, or fated, depending on how you look at it. Well before the news of this distinction came to Willy, Huron had been on his mind. While designing his spring 2026 collection, he took a road trip to his hometown to film a short film, the first time he’d returned in some 20 years, and the experience was deeply moving. Learn more about the celebrations at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on May 07, 2026. May be an image of one or more people and text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY WILLY CHAVARRIA'S HOMETOWN NAMES A DAY IN HIS HONOR'.

posts.caption:
Never one to make a timid entrance, @Madonna made a big statement on the 2026 #MetGala red carpet. There was the witchy dress by @ysl, the tumble of raven hair, and the seven ladies-in-waiting carrying her diaphonous lavender cape, a reinterpretation of the 1945 oil painting “The Temptation of St. Anthony,” by one of the singer’s favorite artists, the British-Mexican Surrealist Leonora Carrington. All of this seemingly in service of the dramatic centerpiece: a towering headpiece featuring a skeletal ship dripping in tulle and beads. The hat once belonged to the infamous fashion editor Isabella Blow, and was commissioned by her from fashion’s go-to milliner, Philip Treacy, who says it’s among his favorite creations.
Tap the link in our bio for all the details, including more on  the history the chapeau has with Vogue itself.

posts.caption:
✨ Creator, Actor, PHENOMENON! ✨ On the June-July cover of Wedding Vows, we present the trailblazing @mostlysane, Prajakta Koli!
From building a YouTube empire to being the lead for Netflix Original Mismatched, from being part of Michelle Obama’s Day Time Emmy winning docu-series to being UNDP India's First Youth Climate Champion, her journey is nothing short of extraordinary.
With her debut romance novel set to launch this year, along with her first Amazon Prime Original Andhera, Prajakta’s journey is a testament to the power of dreams, hard work, and staying true to oneself. She continues to inspire millions with her authenticity, relatability, and unstoppable spirit.

posts.caption:
𝐘𝐨𝐮 𝐝𝐢𝐝𝐧'𝐭 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭 𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐜𝐞𝐬. 𝐘𝐨𝐮 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐡𝐞𝐫 𝐛𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐟.⁣ 𝐍𝐨 𝐛𝐮𝐝𝐠𝐞𝐭. 𝐍𝐨 𝐛𝐚𝐜𝐤𝐮𝐩. 𝐉𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐚 𝐦𝐨𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 𝐰𝐡𝐨 𝐬𝐚𝐢𝐝 - 𝐘𝐨𝐮 𝐜𝐚𝐧.⁣

To every mother being celebrated at a wedding this season — you are the reason the shaadi exists, you are the OG wedding planner.
To every mother in this industry — the biggest warrior in the world is you.

posts.caption:
@maudeapatow is ready now. Fresh off her return in the highly anticipated third season of #Euphoria, Apatow is stepping into a new chapter with the kind of clarity and control that only comes from growing up in real time—on-screen, off-screen, and alongside co-stars who have gone on to navigate intergalactic levels of fame and careers of their own. "Just thinking about us being little kids and having no idea what was in store to now… It's insane," she says.
In our May cover story, Apatow opens up about the roles that shaped her, the relationships that fulfilled her, the theater work that grounded her, and the rituals—travel, matcha, and all—that keep her moving forward. Read our full interview with the actress and director at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
Ralph Lauren began his namesake brand nearly six decades ago in 1967. He went from designing neckties that revolutionized the standardized styles of the time to launching his first full line of womenswear inspired by his wife and muse, Ricky Lauren.
In the 59 years since, Ralph Lauren has become one of the most influential American fashion designers, defining aspirational style and living and allowing so many people to see themselves in his vision of the American dream. Ralph Lauren: Catwalk features more than 100 womenswear collections and 1300 runway looks from the brand's archives. For more on the recently-released book, tap the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
Watches and Wonders really lived up to the second half of its name this year.
The annual expo in Geneva, Switzerland, brings together the watch industry’s biggest players to unveil their most wondrous, whimsical, and world-class creations.
Last year, I attended the convention in person, where I got to try on all the new watches by Chopard, Cartier, TAG Heuer, Piaget, and more. Not many people can say they wore something before it graced the steps of the Met Gala, but that was exactly the case with a new Cartier Panthère design I tried on at Watches and Wonders in 2025 that debuted on Jon Batiste on the famed red carpet a month later.
#womenculture #womenwatch #luxurywatch

posts.caption:
Happy Mother's Day! ⁠
⁠
From Jeanne Lanvin’s early “copy to copy” creations to the rise of today’s “Mommy & Me” dressing, mother-daughter style has long been woven into fashion history. This 1952 WWD clipping captures the enduring appeal of generational dressing — a tradition that continues to inspire runways, campaigns and Mother’s Day wardrobes decades later. ⁠
⁠
Read more from our archives at WWD.com — #linkinbio ⁠

posts.caption:
Chanel’s next Métiers d’Art destination: Rome. ⁠
⁠
The house will present Matthieu Blazy’s upcoming collection in the Italian capital on Dec. 2, spotlighting the craftsmanship of its famed artisan ateliers and the enduring influence of Italy on Coco Chanel’s world.⁠
⁠
The announcement was accompanied by a photograph of founder Gabrielle Chanel with Italian filmmaker Luchino Visconti (swipe to see). The two worked together on his segment of the comedy anthology movie “Boccaccio ’70,” released in 1962, with Chanel designing the costumes for the film’s star, Romy Schneider.⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠

@coach is expanding its @wnba presence with a new partnership alongside the @nyliberty team.
The Liberty is one of the league’s three original franchises and is currently prepping for its 30th season, which will kick off on Friday.
As reported, Coach is the official handbag partner of the WNBA, a deal that is now entering its second year.
Expect Story Sessions, a Courtside Book Club and plenty of Tabby bag moments at Barclays Center this season.
